summary: Dick Wright was a black boy down in Mississippi whose experience I had never shared before. Unlike me, he felt invisible and faced the harsh realities of racial discrimination head-on. His life in Mississippi was a stark contrast to my sheltered upbringing, and understanding his perspective helped me realize the depth of racial injustice in the South.

Now, here I am in Mississippi working on a literacy program for Negro sharecroppers in the Delta, helping them register to vote. The conditions are harsh—no real schools, parents mostly illiterate, and poverty everywhere. It’s grassroots work, starting from zero, trying to inch up from mud and dust, with hostile police and deep fear among the people.

I never felt invisible like Ralph Ellison did, though I understood his sense of being nothing. Reading about his experience made me realize how sheltered I had been growing up. His perspective opened my eyes to the deeper struggles faced by many African Americans beyond my own middle-class experience.

I had never seen a Klansman or witnessed a race murder, though I knew such things happened. The Ku Klux Klan was part of the dark reality I pushed out of my mind growing up. Their presence was a shadow over the South, but it was something I had only read about, not experienced directly until later.

I knew about Negro slums like Harlem and the South Side of Chicago, but I thought about them the same way most white boys think about slums. It was a distant reality, not part of my daily life. Harlem represented a place of struggle and poverty that I was aware of but didn't fully grasp until later.

Like Harlem, the South Side of Chicago was known to me as a place of Negro slums. I viewed it as a place of bad housing and hardship, similar to how white kids think about their own slums. It was part of the broader picture of racial segregation and poverty that I was only beginning to understand.

Before we started our own sit-in, we read up on what others had done in Greensboro. Their courage and strategy inspired us to take action in our town. The Greensboro sit-ins were a pivotal example of nonviolent protest that shaped our approach.

We also studied the Tallahassee bus boycott as part of preparing for our sit-ins. It was important to understand the power of sustained nonviolent resistance. The boycott showed us how collective action could challenge segregation and discrimination effectively.

We didn't have time to read Martin Buber before our sit-ins, but his ideas about dialogue and human relations were part of the intellectual background we aspired to. His philosophy on understanding the other person influenced the nonviolent approach we tried to embody.

Though we didn't get to read Gandhi directly before our sit-ins, his example of nonviolent resistance was central to our movement. We learned from others about his strategic use of compassion and shrewdness in fighting oppression. Gandhi's legacy gave us a model for positive nonviolence and redemption through suffering.

We didn't have time to read Thoreau before starting our sit-ins, but his ideas about civil disobedience were part of the broader philosophy guiding us. His writings on standing up to unjust laws helped shape the moral foundation of our actions.

We took time to read Martin Luther King Jr.'s writings on redemption through suffering and nonviolence before our sit-ins. His emphasis on absorbing cruelty with compassion and conciliation deeply influenced our approach. King's vision of love and forgiveness was a guiding light during the toughest moments of our protests.

I remember Dan saying there was nothing about redemption in Franny and Zooey, which we had read. It was a contrast to the ideas we found in King's writings. The book didn't prepare us for the spiritual and moral challenges of our movement, but it was part of our reading as we tried to understand what lay ahead.

Someone suggested we read Camus' The Fall to understand redemption better, but we decided we didn't have time. Camus' existential themes were intriguing but felt less immediate than the need to act. Still, his work was part of the intellectual conversation around our struggle.

The Fall by Camus was recommended to us as a way to explore redemption, but we postponed reading it to focus on our sit-ins. Its themes of judgment and moral responsibility resonated with our experiences, even if we hadn't fully engaged with the text yet. It lingered in our minds as something to return to after the movement gained momentum.

Our first sit-in was at Walgreens, though I don't know why we picked that store. We thought a chain might be more sensitive to pressure. Sitting at the lunch counter, reading our books, we faced hostility but stayed nonviolent even when a cigarette was burned into my back. That moment marked the real start of our revolution.

After Walgreens, we moved on to sit-ins at Kress's, where the movement grew to include about twenty-five students and many high school kids. The atmosphere was brave and joyful, with laughter and singing despite the challenges. Kress's eventually opened its lunch counter to everyone, marking a significant victory for us.

A worker from the Congress of Racial Equality came to train us in nonviolence. He taught us about compassion, forgiveness, and absorbing evil through our own suffering. CORE's guidance helped us maintain discipline and believe in the possibility of redeeming even those who opposed us.

In college, we read Waiting for Godot, but it didn't prepare us for the harsh realities we faced later. The play's theme of waiting seemed to reflect the lives of the Negro children in the Delta, who seemed to be waiting endlessly for change. It was a haunting metaphor for the struggle ahead.

The United States is the backdrop for all our struggles and hopes. From the segregated South to the North, we saw the need to open up the whole country to justice and equality. The children in the Delta, the sit-ins in our town, and the broader civil rights movement all reflect the ongoing fight to make America live up to its promises.
